title: The Light in the Old House
summary: Anna sees a light in the old house next door at night.
coverScene: a small warm house at night beside a tall old house whose single window glows softly, under a starry sky

sentences.text: Anna lives in a small town.
She lives with her mother.
Next to her house is an old house.
No one lives in the old house.
It is dark and quiet every day.
One night, Anna looks out at the house.
She sees a light in the house.
The light moves from room to room.
Then it goes out.
Anna watches for a long time.
The house is dark again.
The light comes back every night at ten.
Anna tells her mother about the light.
Her mother says it is a dream.
But Anna knows it is not a dream.
In the day, she looks at the house.
The door is closed.
It looks quiet and old.
Anna asks an old man about the house.
He says no one lives there now.
He says it is the Marsh house.
The house has many old books in it.
People do not go in there.
Anna wants to know about the light.
One night she walks to the old house.
The door is open.
She goes in very quietly.
The house is full of old books.
There are books on every table.
Then Anna hears a sound.
It comes from up in the house.
Her heart goes fast, but she goes up.
She opens a door slowly.
An old woman sits there with a light.
She is reading an old book.
The woman smiles at Anna.
Her name is Ellen.
"This is my family house," she says.
"I come at night to read our books."
"I do not want people to see me."
Anna understands her.
Now they read together every week.
The old house is not dark now.

options: In a small town
In a big city
On a farm

quiz.q: What does Anna see at night?
options: A light in the old house
A dog in the garden
A car near the house

quiz.q: Who reads books in the old house?
options: Ellen
Anna's mother
An old man

sentences.text: Anna lives with her mother in a small town.
Next to their house there is an old house.
Nobody lives in it, and the garden is full of tall grass.
The windows are dark, and the door is always closed.
People in the town call it the Marsh house.
One night, Anna looked out of her window before bed.
There was a small light in the old house.
It moved slowly from room to room.
Then it stopped, and the house was dark again.
Anna watched for a long time, but nothing happened.
The light came back every night at ten.
Anna told her mother about it.
"You are dreaming," her mother said with a smile.
But Anna knew it was not a dream.
She sat by her window every night that week.
The little light always came back at the same time.
It moved like a slow star in the dark house.
In the day the house looked quiet, old, and sad.
Anna asked Mr Reed, an old man from her street.
"Nobody has lived there for twenty years," he said.
"Miss Marsh lived there with her books."
"She loved books more than anything."
"The house is still full of them."
Anna thought about the light and the books all day.
One night, Anna decided to look.
She walked through the garden to the old house.
She was going to knock, but the door was open.
She went in very quietly.
There were books everywhere, on every table and chair.
The air smelled of old paper.
Then she heard a sound from up in the house.
Her heart went fast, but she walked up slowly.
A door was half open, and light came from it.
She pushed the door and looked in.
An old woman sat by a small light, reading.
She did not look surprised at all.
"Hello," the woman said.
"My name is Ellen Marsh."
"This was my sister's house, and our family books are here."
"I live far away now, but I come back at night."
"I read our old books and remember my sister."
"People talk too much, so I come quietly."
Anna smiled and sat down next to her.
They read together until it was very late.
Now Anna visits Ellen every week.
They are going to read every book in the house.
The light in the old house is not strange now.
It is the light of a friend.

quiz.q: Why is the old house dark in the day?
options: Nobody lives there
The windows are broken
Anna closes the door

quiz.q: What did Mr Reed say about Miss Marsh?
options: She loved books
She loved music
She loved gardens

quiz.q: Why does Ellen come at night?
options: She does not want people to see her
She works in the day
The house is cold in the day

sentences.text: Anna has lived in the same small town all her life.
Her house stands next to an old empty house with a wild garden.
Everyone calls it the Marsh house, and everyone walks past it quickly.
The windows are always dark, and the gate makes a sad sound in the wind.
Children in the town tell each other strange stories about it.
One night in October, Anna was closing her window before bed.
Suddenly she saw a small yellow light inside the old house.
It moved slowly from room to room, like someone walking with a lamp.
After a few minutes it stopped, and the house was dark again.
Anna stood at her window for a long time, watching and thinking.
The light returned every night at exactly ten o'clock.
Anna began to keep a small notebook by her window.
She wrote down the time, the weather, and what the light did.
After seven nights, the notes all said the same thing.
The light arrived at ten, moved through three rooms, and died at eleven.
Anna has never believed in ghosts, but this was hard to explain.
When she told her mother, her mother laughed and said it was a dream.
"If you watch that house every night, you will start seeing things," she said.
But Anna knew what she had seen, and she wanted an answer.
The next day she visited Mr Reed, the oldest man on her street.
He has lived in the town for more than eighty years.
"Nobody has lived in that house for twenty years," he told her.
"Miss Marsh was the last one, and she loved books more than people."
"When she died, her family left everything inside, even her library."
"If those books could speak, they would tell a hundred stories."
That night, Anna made a decision that surprised even her.
She waited until her mother had gone to sleep.
Then she put on her coat and took a small light with her.
The night air was cold, and the town around her was silent.
At ten o'clock she crossed the wild garden with quiet steps.
She was planning to knock, but then she saw the open door.
Inside, the air smelled of dust and old paper.
Books covered every table, every chair, and most of the floor.
Somewhere above her, a floor board made a soft sound.
Her heart was beating fast as she climbed the stairs.
A warm light came from a half open door at the top.
She pushed it gently and looked inside the room.
An old woman sat in a big chair with a book on her knees.
She looked up calmly, as if she had been waiting for Anna.
"You have finally come," she said with a small smile.
The woman's name was Ellen, and she was Miss Marsh's younger sister.
She had moved to the city many years ago, before her sister died.
"I have read these books since I was a child," she explained.
"Every one of them still smells like my sister's tea."
"I come at night because I do not want questions from the town."
"People love a mystery more than they love the truth."
Anna stayed that night and listened to stories about the two sisters.
Ellen told her how they had read under the table as children.
She showed Anna her sister's favorite book, full of little notes.
Since then, Anna has visited Ellen every Friday evening.
Together they are slowly reading through the whole library.
If they finish it one day, Ellen says, they will simply start again.
If anyone asks about the light now, Anna just smiles.
Some mysteries are better as friends than as stories.

quiz.q: What did the moving light look like to Anna?
options: Someone walking with a lamp
A fire starting in a room
A car light on the wall

quiz.q: What happened to the house after Miss Marsh died?
options: Her family left everything inside
The town sold all the books
Mr Reed moved into it

quiz.q: Why does Ellen visit only at night?
options: She wants to avoid questions from the town
She works every day in the city
The house is too hot in the day

sentences.text: Anna had grown up believing that nothing interesting ever happened in her town.
It was the kind of place where every face was familiar and every day repeated the last.
The only exception was the Marsh house, which stood beside her own like a forgotten museum.
Its garden had been swallowed by wild grass, and its gate cried quietly whenever the wind passed.
The house had been empty for twenty years, or at least that was what everyone believed.
On a cold October night, as she was closing her window, Anna noticed something impossible.
A soft yellow light was moving through the dark rooms of the empty house.
It traveled slowly from window to window, paused in the highest room, and then died.
She waited for almost an hour, but the darkness that followed was complete.
If she had seen it only once, she might have accepted her mother's explanation.
But the light returned the next night, and the night after that, always at ten.
Her mother, who had little patience for mysteries, said that tired eyes invent strange things.
"If you spent less time at that window, you would sleep better," she said.
Anna disagreed, though she kept her disagreement to herself.
She began keeping careful notes, recording the hour the light appeared and the path it followed.
By the end of the week, her notebook told a story that no dream could explain.
The visitor always entered the same three rooms, in the same order, for the same length of time.
She had watched the light for a week, and it moved with purpose, not like a dream.
Whoever carried it knew the house well enough to walk it in the dark.
The history of the house was explained to her by Mr Reed, her oldest neighbor.
He was a man who collected the town's memories the way others collect stamps.
"The last owner was Miss Marsh, a woman who preferred books to almost everyone," he said.
"She built a library that was famous in three towns, and she guarded it like treasure."
"When she died, the family locked the doors and left every single book inside."
"Buyers came from the city more than once, and every one of them was refused."
"It was said that her sister never forgave herself for moving away."
Anna asked whether anyone had a key to the house.
Mr Reed smiled and said that old houses decide for themselves who may enter.
At the time, Anna thought this was simply the way old men liked to talk.
That evening, Anna decided that the mystery had been allowed to grow long enough.
She waited in the dark kitchen until her mother's door closed upstairs.
The town outside was so quiet that she could hear her own steps on the road.
At exactly ten o'clock, she crossed the wet garden, guided by the light above.
She had prepared a polite knock and an apology, but neither was needed.
The heavy front door, which should have been locked for twenty years, stood open.
Inside, the air was thick with dust and the sweet, tired smell of old paper.
Books were piled on every surface, as if the house itself had been built from them.
A board moved somewhere above her head, and her courage nearly left her.
She climbed the stairs slowly, one hand on the cold wall, counting her own breaths.
At the top, warm light escaped from a door that had been left half open.
She pushed it and found a small room that the light had made golden.
An old woman sat in a deep chair, a book resting on her knees.
"You took your time," she said calmly, as if Anna had been invited weeks ago.
Her name was Ellen Marsh, and she was the younger sister Mr Reed had mentioned.
She had left for the city as a young woman, chasing a job and a marriage.
Both had ended, but her sister had died before Ellen found the courage to return.
"This library was our whole childhood," she said, touching the page like an old photograph.
"If I had come back one year earlier, I would have heard her voice again."
"Instead, I visit at night and let the books speak for her."
She explained that she still kept a key that her sister had given her long ago.
Once a week she drove out from the city, opened the quiet house, and read until midnight.
"The town would turn me into a story, and I have no interest in being one," she said.
"A woman reading at night becomes a ghost very quickly in a small town."
Anna stayed until midnight, listening to stories that no book in the room contained.
Ellen described two girls who had read under the kitchen table while their parents slept.
She showed Anna the notes her sister had written in the corners of her favorite pages.
Some of the notes were jokes, and some were small letters to nobody at all.
Anna understood then that a library can hold a whole family, long after the family has gone.
She has kept Ellen's secret ever since, guarding it the way Miss Marsh guarded her library.
Every Friday evening, two figures can be seen reading behind the old windows, though nobody looks closely.
When neighbors mention the strange light, Anna agrees that it is probably nothing.
Some truths, she has learned, are too gentle to survive being told.

quiz.q: Why did Anna reject her mother's explanation of the light?
options: The light moved with purpose and returned at the same hour
Her mother had never seen the house
Mr Reed told her the house was dangerous

quiz.q: What did Miss Marsh's family do after her death?
options: They locked the house with the library inside
They sold the books to three towns
They gave the house to Mr Reed

quiz.q: Why does Ellen regret her years in the city?
options: Her sister died before she found the courage to return
She lost the family's money there
She forgot how to read the old books
